journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Nutrition, Health & Aging

title-group.article-title: The effects of creatine and whey protein supplementation on body composition in men aged 48 to 72 years during resistance training

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Nutritional Sciences, University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center, Box 26901, 73190, Oklahoma City, Oklahoma
2Department of Health and Exercise Science, University of Oklahoma, Norman, Oklahoma

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Objectives: Creatine and whey protein are supplements believed to have an ergogenic effect. Very little is known regarding the effects of these dietary supplements in older men. The purpose of this study was to determine the effect of creatine and whey protein supplements, consumed independently and in combination, on total and regional body composition in middle-aged men during a resistance-training program.Design, Setting,Participants: Forty-two men were randomly assigned to four groups to receive supplements according to a double-blind protocol. Groups consumed their supplements three times per week immediately following their resistance training sessions. The groups were: 1) placebo (480 ml of Gatorade®); 2) creatine (480 ml of Gatorade® plus 5 grams of creatine); 3) whey protein (480 ml of Gatorade® plus 35 grams of whey protein powder); and 4) whey protein/creatine (480 ml of Gatorade® plus 5 grams of creatine and 35 grams of whey protein powder). All groups participated in resistance training 3 times per week for 14 weeks.Measurements: At the beginning and end of the study, total and regional measures of body composition (DXA) and total (TBW), intracellular (ICW), and extracellular (ECW) body water (Multifrequency BIA) were measured and 3-day diet records were completed.Results: There were significant training effects for regional arm fat (decrease), regional arm bone free-fat free mass (BF-FFM-increase), total body BF-FFM (increase), ICW (increase), and ECW (increase) but no significant group effects and only one significant group by training interaction (ECW). There were no significant changes for total calorie, carbohydrate, fat or protein intake for any of the groups from prestudy to post-study testing.Conclusion: The results from this study suggest that supplementation with creatine, whey protein, or a combination of creatine and whey protein, when combined with resistance training in middle-aged men, have no added benefit to changes that occur to body composition due to resistance training alone.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: For centuries athletes have relied upon foods and supplements to help them achieve better performance. Two of the most popular ergogenic aids used by athletes today are creatine monohydrate (hereafter referred to as creatine) and whey protein. Numerous studies have documented an increase in both strength and muscle mass when young subjects are supplemented with creatine (1, 2). Whey protein is a combination of beta-lactalbumin, alpha-lactalbumin, blood protein, lactose and minerals. Whey protein contains the highest concentration of the branched chain amino acids isoleucine, leucine and valine of common dietary proteins (3). Many believe supplementation of these branched chain amino acids reduce the breakdown of muscle tissue and must be present in muscle cells for protein synthesis (3). Others suggest that there is no benefit of taking supplements rich in branched chain amino acids (4). While whey protein has been marketed to those wishing to gain muscle mass and strength, only one study has reported an ergogenic benefit of whey protein, observed only when supplemented in combination with creatine (5).

Questions also remain as to the effectiveness of ergogenic aids in the middle-aged population. Studies to date have investigated creatine use in older adults (6, 7, 8, 9), protein supplementation in older adults (10, 11), and resistance training in older adults (10, 12, 13, 14, 15). However, no research to our knowledge has been conducted to evaluate the effect of creatine and whey protein on body composition in an older population during resistance training.

The purpose of this study was to assess changes in measures of total and regional body composition in middle-aged men taking creatine and whey protein supplements, consumed independently and in combination, during a resistance-training program.

sec.title: Study Sample
sec.p: The subject population consisted of 42 males ages 48 to 72 years old. Participants were randomly assigned to one of four groups in a double-blind research design. All four groups followed the same resistance training protocol. The four groups received one of the following four dietary supplements: 1) placebo; 2) creatine; 3) whey protein; and 4) whey protein/creatine combined. Both the placebo and creatine groups had 10 subjects each while the whey protein and whey protein/creatine groups had 11 subjects each. A minimal sample size of 10 per group was determined adequate in order to achieve a statistical power of 0.80. The study was approved by the University Institutional Review Board.

sec.title: Resistance Training
sec.p: Training sessions were supervised and lasted for 14 weeks. Each group met three times per week and participants performed 8 resistance exercises for 3 sets of 8 repetitions at 80% 1RM. The exercises included knee extension (quadriceps) and flexion (hamstrings), elbow extension (triceps) and flexion (biceps), shoulder press (deltoids), lat pull-downs (latissimus dorsi), seated leg-press (hips and quads), and supine bench press (chest). Exercise loads were adjusted every fifth week in order to ens ure progres sive overload was maintained throughout the training period.

sec.title: Supplementation
sec.p: Supplementation began with a one-week loading phase. Groups receiving creatine (creatine and whey protein/creatine) received 7 g creatine in powdered form mixed with 480 mL of Gatorade® sports drink. Groups who were not receiving creatine (placebo and whey protein) received 480 ml of Gatorade® during the loading phase to ensure continuity of the double-blind study design. The creatine loading occurred on Monday, Wednesday and Friday during the one-week loading phase. Upon completion of the loading phase, the supplementation phase began. At this time, all groups received the treatment protocol. Solutions were consumed only on training days and were consumed immediately following the cool-down portion of the strength training (16, 17). The placebo group consumed 480 mL of Gatorade®. The whey protein group received 35 g of whey protein concentrate 80% powder (Old Fashioned Natural Products, Santa Ana, CA) mixed with 480 mL of Gatorade®. The groups receiving creatine (creatine and whey protein/creatine) received 5 g creatine mixed with 480 ml Gatorade®. In addition to the 5 g creatine, the whey protein/creatine group received 35 g whey protein powder mixed with the Gatorade®. Supplements were served in opaque water bottles. Solutions were made fresh each morning by research staff not responsible for strength training or data collection. Researchers responsible for strength training and data collection were blinded to subject group.

sec.title: Dietary Analysis
sec.p: Each subject completed a three-day food diary during the first week of strength training and again the last week of training. A registered dietitian instructed subjects regarding how to complete a food diary, using food models to estimate portion size. Each three-day food diary was analyzed using Food Works 2.0 (McGraw-Hill Company, Dubuque, Iowa). Subjects were asked to make no changes in their eating patterns throughout the 14-week treatment period.

sec.title: Body Composition and Body Water
sec.p: Dual energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA, Lunar DPX-IQ) was used to measure total body and regional (arms, legs, and trunk) composition (fat mass – FM; percent fat - %Fat; and bone free-fat free mass – BF-FFM) one week prior to training and again one week after training ended. The DXA was calibrated each testing day by using quality assurance procedures and to minimize variability between scanning subjects, consistent body positioning, use of anatomical landmarks, and scan speeds were recorded for each subject. Analyses of all the DXA scans were performed by the same technician. Total body (TBW), intracellular (ICW), and extracellular (ECW) body water was measured by multifrequency bioelectric impedance (BIA - Xitron, BIS4000) both before and after the training program (18). Subjects were supine and electrodes were placed at the right ankle and wrist. Three trials were completed and averaged.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Data was analyzed using SPSS 10.0 for Windows. All data was presented as mean ± SE and as percent change [{(post value – pre value)/pre value} X 100] over the treatment period. Comparisons among groups and trials were made using a 2-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with repeated measures. Statistical significance was set at a level of p<0.05.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Table 1 illustrates the actual and percent changes in total body mass (kg), total BF-FFM (kg), and total FM (kg), and total body %Fat from pre-study to post-study testing for the four treatment groups. There were no significant group, training, or group by training interactions for total body mass or total FM. However, there was a significant (p = 0.02) training effect for total body BF-FFM, although there was no significant group or group by training interaction. The supplementation groups all experienced an increase in BF-FFM (creatine: +4.0%, whey protein: +1.1%, and whey protein/creatine: +2.4%) while the placebo group lost BF-FFM (-0.5%). It should also be noted that total body % Fat approached a statistically significant level (p=0.051) for the training effect. The creatine only group lost 2.4% of total body %Fat while each of the other three groups lost 1.2% of total body %Fat.Table 1
Effect of creatine and whey protein supplementation on total body mass (BM), total body bone free-fat free mass (BF-FFM), total body fat mass (FM), and total body percent fat (%Fat)
Pre-studyPost-study% Δ
Placebo
Total BM (kg)98.0 ± 7.698.3 ± 7.2+0.3
Total Body BF-FFM (kg)*68.0 ± 3.867.6 ± 2.2-0.5
Total Body FM (kg)27.5 ± 3.426.9 ± 2.6-2.2
Total Body %Fat27.9 ± 1.727.6 ±1.5-1.2
Creatine
Total BM (kg)91.1 ± 5.292.4 ± 5.4+1.5
Total Body BF-FFM (kg)*62.9 ± 2.265.4 ± 2.3+4.0
Total Body FM (kg)25.8 ± 2.625.5 ± 1.9-1.1
Total Body %Fat28.7 ± 1.427.5 ± 1.4-2.4
Whey Protein
Total BM (kg)88.3 ± 4.488.7 ± 4.2+0.4
Total Body BF-FFM (kg)*64.0 ± 1.964.7 ± 1.8+1.1
Total Body FM (kg)21.6 ± 2.521.6 ± 2.60
Total Body %Fat24.5 ± 1.824.2 ± 1.7-1.2
Whey Protein/Creatine
Total BM (kg)92.6 ± 5.192.5 ± 5.5-0.1
Total Body BF-FFM (kg)*66.9 ± 2.568.5 ± 2.8+2.4
Total Body FM (kg)23.1 ± 2.423.1 ± 2.80
Total Body %Fat25.1 ± 1.524.8 ± 1.6-1.2
Values are means ± SE. *Significant training effect for BF-FFM (p = 0.02), no significant group or group by training interactions. % Δ = percent change from pre- to post-testing.

Table 2 provides the changes for the arms, legs, and trunk for each measure of regional body composition (BF-FFM (kg); and %Fat) for each of the four groups. There was a significant training effect for both the arms (p = 0.001) and legs (p = 0.02) BF-FFM and for the arms %Fat (p = 0.001). Arm BF-FFM significantly increased for each of the four groups following the 14-week training program. The lack of a group or group by training interaction indicates that each of the four groups improved regardless of the supplement condition.Table 2
Effect of creatine and whey protein supplementation on regional measures of bone free-fat free mass (BF-FFM), and percent fat (%Fat)
Pre-studyPost-study% Δ
Placebo

Trunk - %Fat28.2 ± 1.528.1 ± 1.3-0.4

Arms - BF-FFM (kg)a7.2 ± 0.27.6 ± 0.2+6.2
Arms - %Fatb25.8 ± 2.723.8 ± 1.9-8.4

Legs - %Fat28.0 ± 1.626.8 ± 1.0-4.5
Trunk - BF-FFM (kg)32.1 ± 1.733.5 ± 2.1+4.5
Trunk - %Fat29.7 ± 1.129.0 ± 1.0-2.4
Whey Protein

Arms - %Fatb20.7 ± 2.020.3 ± 2.2-2.0
Legs - BF-FFM (kg)c21.0 ± 0.521.4 ± 0.9+2.0
Legs - %Fat23.6 ± 1.923.4 ± 2.0-0.9
Trunk - BF-FFM (kg)32.2 ± 1.532.0 ± 2.0-0.4
Trunk - %Fat26.1 ± 1.825.9 ± 1.6-0.8
Whey Protein/Creatine
Arms - BF-FFM (kg)a7.9 ± 0.38.4 ± 0.5+6.8
Arms - %Fatb22.2 ± 1.921.1 ± 2.0-5.2
Legs - BF-FFM (kg)c22.0 ± 0.922.6 ± 0.7+2.6
Legs - %Fat23.9 ± 1.724.0 ± 1.5+0.4

Trunk - %Fat26.7 ± 1.426.5 ± 0.9-0.8
Values are means ± SE. a. Significant training effect (increase) for Arms BF-FFM (p = 0.001), no significant group or group by training interactions. b. Significant training effect (decrease) for Arms %Fat (p = 0.001), no significant group or group by training interactions. c. Significant training effect (increase) for Legs BF-FFM (p = 0.02), no significant group or group by training interactions. % Δ = percent change from pre- to post-testing.

The other statistically significant finding concerning regional body composition measures occurred for the %Fat in the arms. %Fat in the arms decreased significantly for each of the four groups regardless of the supplementation status (placebo: - 4.4%; creatine: -8.4%; whey protein: -2.0%; and whey protein/creatine: -5.2%). Generally, %Fat decreased in the arms, legs, and trunk while BF-FFM increased in each group.

Table 3 shows the effects of creatine and whey protein supplementation on TBW, ICW, and ECW. There was a significant training effect for ICW (p=0.041) and ECW (p=0.004). There was also a significant group by training interaction for ECW (p=0.029), with both the placebo (+4.3%) and creatine only groups (+3.7%) having larger increases in ECW than the whey protein only (-1.0%) and whey protein/creatine groups (+1.0%).Table 3
Effect of creatine and whey protein supplementation on total body water (TBW), intracellular water (ICW), and extracellular water (ECW)

ECW (L)*a21.1 ± 1.322.0 ± 0.9+4.3

Values are means ± SE. *Significant training effect (increase) for ICW (p = 0.041) and ECW (p = 0.004), no significant group effect. a. Significant group by training interaction for ECW (p = 0.029). % Δ = percent change from pre- to post-testing.

The average caloric intake from food was not significantly different for the groups at either pre or post-testing, nor did average caloric intake from food change from pre to post-testing in any group (Table 4). The average daily caloric intake from food for all groups was 2000 kcal. Average diet composition, excluding supplements, for all groups was 46% carbohydrate, 37% fat and 17% protein, with no significant differences observed between the groups or from pre to post-testing (Table 4).Table 4
Dietary caloric intake, excluding supplements, of the four treatment groups

CHO kcal, PRO kcal, and FAT kcal = kcalories (and % of total kcalories) from carbohydrate, protein, and fat, respectively. Values do not reflect calories from supplements. Values are means ± SE. No significant between group effect existed (p = 0.05). No significant training effect existed (p = 0.05).

Total protein intake data are presented in Table 5. Total protein intake was determined by adding the g of protein from the whey protein supplement to the protein intake reported from the three-day food diary. The groups with the highest total protein intakes at the post-study testing were the whey protein group, which consumed 130% of the RDA for protein, and the whey protein/creatine group, which consumed 156% of the RDA for protein.Table 5
Total protein intake, including supplements, of subjects both at pre- and post-testing

% RDA = percent of RDA needs met by protein intake (0.8 g protein / kg body weight). Values include study-administered supplements. Values are means ± SE. No significant
No subject discontinued use of the supplements because of discomfort or gastrointestinal complaint. No significant side effects were reported by any of the subjects during the study.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The primary aim of this study was to assess changes in total and regional measures of body composition in middle-aged men taking creatine monohydrate and whey protein supplements, independently and in combination, during a resistance training program. Previous research indicates that creatine supplementation, usually combined with resistance training, is effective in increasing total mass and fat free mass (19, 20). However, only two studies were performed specifically with an older population of men (7, 8). Both studies found that creatine supplementation increased total body and fat-free mass. Protein supplementation combined with resistance training has also been reported to increase fat-free mass in older men (10, 21). The results of the current study found no significant group, training, or group by training interactions for total body mass. There was a significant training effect for total BF-FFM, indicating that total body muscle mass increased after the 14-week training period, but that the increases were similar in all four treatment groups. Creatine and whey protein supplementation, both independently and in combination, had no effect on fat-free mass in this study.

Burke et al. (5) observed a significant increase in lean tissue mass with whey protein supplementation in young men. In that study, subjects received 1.2 g of whey protein per kg body weight as a supplement. The rate of supplementation in the present study was significantly lower (0.23-0.24 g per kg body weight). The level of 35 g whey protein powder supplemented in this study was the maximal amount that readily dissolved in 480 ml of solution, a requirement to maintain the double-blind research design.

The age of the subject population in this study may have prevented changes in body mass due to creatine and whey protein supplementation. Several studies suggest that creatine supplementation benefits the body composition of young men and that supplemental protein will increase fat free mass in this population (19, 20). The subjects in the present study may not have responded to the creatine and whey protein supplements because the rate of muscle synthesis is lower in older men due to a decrease in testosterone (22) and myosin heavy chain protein synthesis (23). The supplementation dose that is effective in changing body composition in young men may not be sufficient to affect older men.

The Gatorade© drink contained 100 calories and the whey protein contained an additional 105 calories per supplement. When this combination was taken 3 times per week, the subjects in the whey protein supplemented group received an additional 615 calories per week, while the placebo and creatine only groups received an additional 300 calories per week. This additional caloric intake was apparently offset by the additional energy expenditure from the resistance training, resulting in little or no body weight gain.

It has been suggested that an increase in TBW due to creatine supplementation may promote muscle protein hypertrophy (24). Studies (1, 18) have supported this suggestion and shown significant increases in ICW and ECW with creatine supplementation. In the present study, all four groups experienced a significant increase in ICW following training, ranging from 1.2% to 6.5%, and all groups except the whey protein only group had significant increases in ECW.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Creatine and whey protein supplementation had no effect on body composition changes seen in middle-aged men doing resistance training. Since this is the first known study to investigate the effects of these supplements in this population, further studies should be conducted before disregarding the possible benefits of these supplements.
